slides.title: Unbounded Danger ⚠️
slides.content:
A generic type `<T>` means *literally any type*. What if we write a function `print_loudly<T>(item: T)` that tries to print the item? 

Since not all types know how to be printed to the console, the compiler will panic and throw an error!

slides.title: Setting Boundaries 🔗
slides.content:
We need a way to tell the compiler: 'This generic `<T>` can be anything... **as long as** it has a specific set of abilities.'

We do this using **Trait Bounds**.

slides.title: Applying a Bound 🧱
slides.content: We restrict `<T>` using a colon followed by the Trait we require, like `std::fmt::Display` (which allows printing).

quizData.question: What is the specific purpose of a Trait Bound (`<T: Summary>`) in Rust?

options.text: To force the generic type to use less memory
To strictly guarantee at compile time that the generic generic type legitimately uniquely implements a specific required Trait (behavior)

To cleanly explicitly completely bypass natively firmly efficiently securely natively perfectly seamlessly deeply safety cleanly organically intelligently realistically explicit exactly checks gracefully elegantly.
